kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 03-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL cash $3,080.00 vs 02-Jul $3,061.50 = +$18.50 (+0.6%) — the first up-day since 26-Jun, closing back ABOVE the $3,077 (.50 retrace). Still ~20% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 03-Jul official $3,087.00 vs 02-Jul $3,065.00 (+$22.00, +0.7%). After two closes marginally below the .50 retracement ($3,077), price reclaimed the level — a basing attempt at key support. Curve a shallow -$7.00 contango.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 03-Jul official 298,775 t (Westmetall) — another 1,500 t draw and the FIRST close below the ~300kt days-of-cover threshold this cycle. ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.2 days of global output; steady draws through the correction keep the structural-deficit signal intact.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 03-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL cash $3,080.00 vs 02-Jul $3,061.50 = +$18.50 (+0.6%) — first gain in five sessions and a close back above the $3,077 (.50 retrace) after two closes marginally below it. ~20% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 final monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 03-Jul official $3,087.00 vs 02-Jul $3,065.00, +$22.00 (+0.7%). The reclaim of the .50 retracement ($3,077) on the first up-day since 26-Jun is a tentative basing signal — confirmation needs follow-through above $3,260 (.382). avg = June-2026 mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 03-Jul official cash $3,080.00 vs 3M $3,087.00 = -$7.00 = shallow CONTANGO, a touch wider than 02-Jul's -$3.50. Acute scarcity fears remain unwound while steady draws (stock now sub-300kt) keep the front end from sagging — no stress signal either way.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: Latest public SMM reference ~$3,055/t excl-VAT (A00 spot ¥24,370). SMM's 03-Jul midday review reports futures continuing to decline with sluggish trading; ingot social inventory fell ~40kt w/w as lower prices drew out buying. LME's Friday bounce narrows the arb; import window stays shut — verify SMM/metal.com Monday.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (Rio Tinto & South32 with at least one Japanese buyer, 03-Jul) — +12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 the market had penciled in, though below initial $460–480 offers (Bloomberg via Mining.com). avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot
premiums.src: Last public reference ~$338; no fresh PRA print over the 04-Jul weekend. The $395 Q3 settlement skews spot risk UPWARD — expect convergence toward the settled level — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: EU duty-unpaid in-whs Rotterdam; easing on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM. No fresh public print over the weekend — verify Fastmarkets MB.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — sticky vs ingot premia — verify Fastmarkets.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts MW US Transaction premium; Section 232 (50%)-driven and structurally elevated. The tariff-$ component (50% x LME) ticks back UP with Friday's LME bounce — verify PRA Monday.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020 ddp Midwest (~$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + 50% tariff) — verify PRA.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: Holding ~100.8 after the June payrolls miss (+57k vs ~110k; Apr/May revised -74k) knocked September hike odds to ~50% from ~64% — the dollar headwind for metals has eased. US markets closed Fri 03-Jul (Independence Day observed); re-validate at Monday's open (BLS / Trading Economics).

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro firmer as the dollar sagged post-payrolls — Westmetall LME MTLE fix 1.14040 (02-Jul); holiday-thinned trade into the weekend.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77 — China export competitiveness intact; the aluminium export ramp continues (Fastmarkets sees +12–18% for 2026), incl. stranded aluminium-wire flows to Korea/Japan (customs data via Bloomberg).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~¥160; intervention risk persists — marginal premium support for Japan-bound cargoes.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: ~4.55 reference; the soft June payrolls print (+57k, u-rate 4.2%) trimmed Fed-hike odds — rate pressure on metals easing at the margin (US bond market closed 03-Jul; re-validate Monday).

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Stabilised ~$70.5–71.7 after the sub-$69 slide — UAE exports restored >3.9 mbpd and Hormuz flows >10 mbpd have unwound the war premium; holiday-thinned session Friday (Trading Economics reference).

macro.name: Natural gas ($/MMBtu)
macro.note: Direct refining input; Gulf gas-disruption risk minimal with transit normalised.

macro.name: Copper ($/lb)
macro.note: LME 3M $13,250/t (02-Jul official, +0.3%) ≈ $6.01/lb — resilient vs the rest of the base complex; +5.9% YTD.

macro.name: Zinc ($/t)
macro.note: LME 3M $3,465/t (02-Jul official) — gave back ground (-1.3%) but still the YTD outperformer (+10.7%).

macro.name: Nickel ($/t)
macro.note: LME 3M $16,330 (03-Jul official, +0.6%) — a modest bounce off 2026 lows; the surplus story is intact (-3.5% YTD).

macro.name: Gold ($/oz)
macro.note: Westmetall London fixing $4,064.20 (02-Jul vs $3,978.55 on 01-Jul) — bid up as the dollar and hike odds slipped post-payrolls.

macro_events: THE BOUNCE AT THE .50: the 03-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL closed $3,080.00 cash / $3,087.00 3M (+0.6/+0.7%) — the first up-day since 26-Jun, reclaiming the $3,077 (.50 retracement) after two closes marginally below it. LME stock broke below 300kt (298,775 t) on steady ~1,500 t/day draws.

JAPAN Q3 PREMIUM SETTLED UP, NOT DOWN: Rio Tinto and South32 agreed Q3 MJP at $395/t with at least one Japanese buyer — above Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus range (initial offers $460–480). The physical market is still short units even as the LME corrects (Bloomberg via Mining.com, 03-Jul).

GULF SUPPLY HEALING SLOWLY: EGA says ~7% of Al Taweelah pots are back online, is exporting from inventories (stockpiles back to normal by end-2026) and aims to accelerate the restoration; ~700kt of Gulf metal remains framed to return in H2 — the bearish swing for the second half.

SECTOR CONSOLIDATION: Alcoa agreed to acquire South32's aluminium portfolio (Worsley Alumina 86%, Hillside Aluminium 100%) for up to $5.6bn — $3.1bn cash + $1bn stock + up to $750m contingent on aluminium prices to 2030 (announced 01-Jul; Reuters/Bloomberg).

MACRO / FREIGHT: June payrolls missed (+57k; DXY ~100.8; Sep hike odds ~50%) — supportive for $-metals; US markets closed Friday for July-4. Dry-bulk freight slid: BDI -1.4% to 2,490 on 03-Jul, a sixth straight decline and the lowest since 15-Apr (capesize -2.8% to 3,538) — easing bauxite/alumina freight costs (Cyprus Shipping News / Baltic Exchange).
lme_commentary: Sat 04-Jul run — the latest OFFICIAL close is Fri 03-Jul: $3,080.00 cash / $3,087.00 3M / stock 298,775 t (Westmetall). The correction stalled and turned exactly at the $3,077 (.50 retracement): after two closes marginally below the level, Friday printed +0.6/+0.7% — the first gain in five sessions — with the curve a shallow -$7.00 contango. Stock draws continue (~1,500 t/day) and the headline count broke below the ~300kt days-of-cover threshold for the first time this cycle. The macro turned supportive into the weekend (June payrolls +57k; DXY ~100.8; Sep hike odds ~50%), and the physical signal is loud: Japan's Q3 premium settled UP at $395/t despite the LME slide. Directional drivers unchanged — Hormuz transit restored, ~700kt of Gulf metal priced to return in H2 (EGA at ~7% of Al Taweelah pots), China output/exports rising — against sub-300kt stocks and continuing China destocking. One up-day is not a confirmed base: a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise the count; a sustained break below $3,050 re-opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618). LME reopens Monday 06-Jul.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia; Q2/H2 oversupply still pressuring — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; easing on the building surplus — verify Platts

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t) softer on Q2/H2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$304/t; FOB Australia Platts futures ~$309.4, -0.5%); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 as Indonesia/India refinery capacity ramps into subdued demand — a building ~1 Mt+ surplus.
Gulf alumina-logistics risk has largely cleared as Hormuz transit normalises (flows >10 mbpd) — ~60% of regional alumina imports transit the Strait, and flows have rebuilt on de-escalation.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); Guinea weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply risk minimal with the strait open; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery restart timeline (~12 months) remains the key supply swing factor.
Net: alumina balance skews to surplus (ample supply caps the price); de-escalation removes the logistics overlay, leaving Guinea bauxite-curb risk as the main floor under raw-material cost.
net_read: THE BOUNCE AT THE .50 MEETS A HOT PREMIUM SETTLEMENT. Aluminium's Hormuz-driven run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) has corrected ~20%, but Friday delivered two counter-signals: the 03-Jul OFFICIAL closed $3,080.00 cash / $3,087.00 3M (+0.6/+0.7%) — the first up-day since 26-Jun, reclaiming the $3,077 (.50 retracement) — and Japan's Q3 MJP premium settled at $395/t, UP from Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 the market expected. The physical market is still short units: LME stock broke below 300kt (298,775 t; ~92% Russian) on steady ~1,500 t/day draws, EGA runs only ~7% of Al Taweelah pots (exporting from inventories), and China keeps destocking (-40kt w/w). Bearish forces remain live: restored Hormuz transit, ~700kt of Gulf metal framed to return in H2, rising China output and +12–18% export projections (incl. stranded-wire flows), and the Alcoa–South32 consolidation reshaping ex-China supply. US premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate; the softer dollar post-payrolls (DXY ~100.8) has eased the macro headwind. One up-day is not a base: confirmation needs follow-through above $3,260 (.382).

feed.text: Weekend check: no LME session (Saturday); Westmetall re-verified the 03-Jul officials unchanged — cash $3,080.00 / 3M $3,087.00 / stock 298,775 t. Hydro announced closure of two US extrusion plants (CA, LA; ~350 jobs) into 2027 — a downstream-demand data point against the tight upstream picture.

Westmetall posted the 03-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,080.00 / 3M $3,087.00 / stock 298,775 t — +0.6/+0.7%, the first up-day since 26-Jun, closing back above the $3,077 (.50) support; stock broke below 300kt for the first time this cycle.

Q3 MJP settled at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32 with at least one Japanese buyer) — +12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus range; initial offers were $460–480 (Bloomberg via Mining.com).
EGA: ~7% of Al Taweelah pots back online; exporting from inventories with stockpiles targeted back to normal by end-2026; aiming to accelerate restoration (CFO interview).
BDI -1.4% to 2,490 — sixth straight decline, lowest since 15-Apr (capesize -2.8% to 3,538) — dry-bulk freight costs easing (Cyprus Shipping News).
US June payrolls +57k vs ~110k expected, u-rate 4.2%, -74k back revisions (BLS) — DXY slid to ~100.8, Sep hike odds to ~50%; the dollar headwind on metals eased into the July-4 weekend.

Alcoa agreed to acquire South32's aluminium portfolio (Worsley 86%, Hillside 100%) for up to $5.6bn ($3.1bn cash + $1bn stock + $750m contingent to 2030) — major ex-China supply consolidation (Reuters/Bloomberg).

SMM: China ingot social inventory fell another ~40kt w/w as the lower price drew out downstream buying; primary output keeps rising on new capacity/restarts and stranded-wire exports flow to Korea/Japan.

news.headline: Q3 MJP SETTLED AT $395/t — Rio Tinto and South32 agreed the third-quarter premium with at least one Japanese buyer at $395 over LME, UP from Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus, though below initial $460–480 offers. Japan's Middle-East dependence is forcing buyers to pay up despite the LME's ~20% correction.

Aluminium bounced at the .50 retracement: the 03-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL closed $3,080.00 cash / $3,087.00 3M (+0.6/+0.7%) — the first up-day since 26-Jun — and LME stock broke below 300kt (298,775 t) on a steady 1,500 t draw.

EGA is speeding up the Al Taweelah restoration: ~7% of production pots are back online, exports continue from inventories, and stockpiles are targeted back to normal by end-2026 (CFO Kildemo). The ~700kt H2 Gulf-return framing stands — the supply floor erodes gradually, not suddenly.

Alcoa agreed to buy South32's aluminium portfolio (Worsley Alumina 86%, Hillside Aluminium 100%) for up to $5.6bn — $3.1bn cash + $1bn stock (+ up to $750m contingent on prices to 2030). A major consolidation of ex-China alumina/aluminium supply announced 01-Jul.

June payrolls missed at +57k (vs ~110k; Apr/May revised -74k; u-rate 4.2%) — DXY eased to ~100.8 and September hike odds fell to ~50% from ~64%. First supportive macro impulse since the correction began; US markets were closed Friday (July-4 observed).
news.source: BLS Employment Situation (02-Jul) — bls.gov; Trading Economics

news.headline: Hydro will close two US aluminium extrusion plants — City of Industry (CA) by early 2027 and Delhi (LA) through Q3-2027, ~350 jobs — citing below-capacity running rates and heavy reinvestment needs. A reminder that record-high US all-in prices (tariff-inflated Midwest) are pressuring downstream demand even as upstream stays tight.
news.source: Hydro press release / Aluminium International Today (this week) — hydro.com/us/global/media/news/2026/hydro-to-close-two-extrusion-plants-in-the-u.s

news.headline: Chinese smelters and merchants are maximising exports of stranded aluminium wire (much of it remelted abroad into ingot), with Korean and Japanese buyers active per customs data; ingot social inventory still fell ~40kt w/w as lower prices drew out buying.
BDI fell a sixth straight session on 03-Jul, -1.4% to 2,490 — the lowest since 15-Apr (capesize -2.8% to 3,538) — easing bulk-freight costs for bauxite/alumina.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's aluminium portfolio (Worsley 86%, Hillside 100%) for up to $5.6bn ($3.1bn cash + $1bn stock + $750m contingent to 2030). Q2 report due ~22-Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due late Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 04-Jul (no new releases in the last 48h). Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 aluminium-portfolio deal (up to $5.6bn) announced 01-Jul. Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa ~22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul; Maaden (1211) late-Jul/early-Aug. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Reuters, Bloomberg, Alcircle, Investing.com.

commercial.text: Friday's bounce off the .50 retrace ($3,077; 3M closed $3,087, +0.7%) is a tentative base, not a confirmed one. Keep LME hedges in place on priced volume; pace unpriced tonnes rather than chasing — a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is the confirmation trigger. Defend value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture: conversion premia stay stickier than ingot premia.

The Q3 MJP settlement at $395/t (vs Q2 $350–353 and a ~$275–320 consensus) resets the Asian benchmark UP — reference the settlement immediately in Q3 renewals and spot offers; CIF Japan spot (~$338 last print) should converge toward it. US MW ~$2,405/t stays structurally high on the 50% Section 232 rate (tariff-$ ticks up with the LME bounce); EU underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM.

Ex-China FOB Australia ~$340/t (API ~$304; Platts futures ~$309) with the Q2/H2 surplus capping upside; Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026. If net seller, transact into any firmness promptly; if buyer for smelting, the surplus plus falling freight (BDI 2,490, six-session slide) offers progressively softer delivered windows.

Normalise routing as Hormuz transit runs >10 mbpd and Gulf exports resume; dry-bulk costs are falling again (BDI -1.4% to 2,490, lowest since 15-Apr) — favourable for bulk freight. Keep war-risk cover and demurrage clauses live while Iran contests maritime administrative control; hold the Jeddah/Red Sea land-bridge on standby until volumes fully recover.

Use the Q3 MJP settlement ($395) to reset the conversation: the physical market remains tight even as the exchange price corrected — push back on discount pressure anchored to the LME slide. Reaffirm reliability and OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and defend premium tolerance through the Q3 reset window.

Watch four confirmations this week: (1) Monday's LME reopen — does $3,077 hold and $3,260 come into play; (2) CIF Japan spot convergence toward the $395 settlement; (3) EGA restart cadence (7% of pots now) and any Alba/Qatalum updates; (4) the Alcoa–South32 completion path and what it signals for ex-China alumina/metal marketing. Q2 earnings season starts mid-July (Alcoa ~22-Jul).

bottom_line: On 04-Jul the story is a tentative turn: Friday's official close ($3,080.00 cash / $3,087.00 3M, +0.6/+0.7%) reclaimed the $3,077 (.50 retracement) on the first up-day since 26-Jun, LME stock broke below 300kt, and Japan's Q3 MJP premium settled at $395/t — up on the quarter and far above consensus — confirming the physical market remains tight even after a ~20% price correction. The bear case is intact (Hormuz restored, ~700kt Gulf return in H2, EGA restart progressing at ~7% of pots, China exports +12–18%), but the structural cushion (sub-300kt stocks, ~92% Russian; Gulf capacity impaired into Q4; China destocking -40kt w/w) argues the $2,950–3,080 zone is support, not air. Macro helps at the margin (payrolls miss; DXY ~100.8; Sep hike odds ~50%). Net: a basing attempt at key support with the premium complex signalling tightness — confirmation needs follow-through above $3,260 (.382); the next catalysts are Monday's LME reopen, CIF Japan spot convergence toward $395, and the mid-July start of Q2 earnings season.

caveats: Report run Sat 04-Jul-2026. Latest LME OFFICIAL on Westmetall is the Fri 03-Jul close (cash $3,080.00 / 3M $3,087.00 / stock 298,775 t); LME is closed over the weekend — next officials Mon 06-Jul.

Copper and zinc Westmetall pages had posted officials only through 02-Jul at run time (Cu 3M $13,250, Zn 3M $3,465, day moves vs 01-Jul); nickel and lead show 03-Jul. The board mixes 02/03-Jul officials accordingly.
US markets were closed Fri 03-Jul (Independence Day observed) — macro references (DXY ~100.8, Brent ~$71, gold fixing $4,064) are the latest public prints and should be re-validated at Monday's open.

PRA premiums (CIF Japan, EDU, billets, US MW) had no fresh public prints over the holiday weekend — values are the latest public references; verify against S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets subscriptions before commercial use.

Q3 MJP settlement ($395/t) is per Bloomberg-sourced trade reporting (Rio Tinto/South32 with at least one buyer); other supplier/buyer pairs may settle at different levels — treat as the benchmark print pending PRA confirmation.

Hormuz status: transit restored (>10 mbpd; UAE >3.9 mbpd) but 'patchy and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights) and Iran still contests maritime administrative control — two-sided headline risk persists.

forward_path.basis: 03-Jul Westmetall official cash; +0.6% vs 02-Jul — first up-day since 26-Jun, back above the $3,077 (.50) support.
03-Jul Westmetall official 3M; +0.7% vs 02-Jul; curve a shallow -$7.00 contango.

outlook.curve_note: The front end sits in shallow contango (-$7.00 on 03-Jul) — acute scarcity fears are unwound while steady ~1,500 t/day stock draws (298,775 t, now BELOW the ~300kt threshold) keep the nearby dates supported. Consensus 2026 average sits in the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired; ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed to return in H2 and China exports run +12–18%, but the Q3 MJP settlement at $395/t shows the physical market is still paying up — the structural deficit remains the floor under the $2,950–3,080 support zone.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; exports seen +12–18% (Fastmarkets), potentially ~7.2 Mt
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)

scenarios.drivers: Gulf re-escalation / Hormuz re-closure, EGA/ALBA restart slips, deficit deepens, China stays capped
drivers_list: Geopolitics: ceasefire breaks down or Hormuz is credibly re-closed — war premium re-ignites
Supply: Gulf restart slips — EGA Al Taweelah / ALBA recovery runs beyond ~12 months, or fresh outages
Inventory: visible LME stocks draw below ~300kt — days-of-cover critical
China: output stays capped at 45 Mt; net exports fall as domestic demand absorbs metal
Targets: Citi/Goldman ~$3,600, upside ~$4,000+ only if the Strait stays shut beyond ~30 days

scenarios.drivers: Ceasefire holds; structural deficit floors price against China/Indonesia supply — elevated but drifting lower
drivers_list: Geopolitics: ceasefire/Hormuz reopening holds — no corridor risk premium
Supply: structural deficit persists (~-365 kt 2026) with Gulf capacity repairing gradually (the floor)
China: exports strong (~+12–18% YoY); output near the 45 Mt cap — the bearish counterweight
Macro: USD firm after the hawkish June FOMC; soft China demand caps rebounds
Curve: contango holds; price oscillates $3,100–3,450 around the support band

scenarios.drivers: Durable de-escalation, China + Indonesia supply surge, hawkish Fed/strong USD, demand softening
drivers_list: Geopolitics: full normalisation — Gulf restarts beat plan, no premium
Supply: China export surge plus rising Indonesian smelter output; Middle East inventories released
Macro: hawkish Fed / stronger USD; rate-hike risk weighs on dollar-priced metals
Demand: global manufacturing & construction soften; destocking sets in
Inventory: visible stocks rebuild as supply returns; contango deepens

risks.risk: China + Indonesia supply (export pace, 45 Mt cap)

catalysts.event: China June trade & aluminium export data
Alcoa Q2 earnings (after close) — sector kickoff
MJP Q3-26 premium SETTLED at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32)

ai_analysis: What changed: the Westmetall OFFICIAL 30-Jun close ($3,105.50 cash / $3,112 3M) posted a fresh -1.7% leg down — the one-session base broke and price extended through the $3,140 shelf into the lower half of the $3,000–$3,170 correction zone.
Term structure: the cash-3M contango edged wider to -$6.50 (from -$5.50) as the front-end softened with the tape — consistent with the geopolitical premium continuing to unwind.

Drivers: de-escalation deepened (Brent <$69, Hormuz flows >10 mbpd, US–Iran talks in Qatar) while China primary output rebounded and 2026 export projections were lifted (+12–18%) — the bearish counterweights. The floor holds: EGA/Alba/Qatalum ~30–60% to Q4, China destocking at a ~4-yr weekly high, LME stock at 303,675 t.

Net base case: price tests the $3,000–$3,080 (.50) support; a sustained break opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618), while a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise. A fresh Gulf re-escalation is the main upside catalyst.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): the Q3 MJP SETTLED at $395/t (+12% q/q; third consecutive rise: $86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395) — producers conceded from $460–480 initial offers but the settlement lands far above the ~$275–320 consensus range, confirming physical tightness. CIF Japan spot (~$338 last reference) should converge upward toward the settlement.
European (EDU): ~$470/t, softer on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM — the policy floor holds even as spot eases.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,405/t; duty-paid P1020A ~$2,040/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff; the tariff-$ component (50% x LME) ticks back up with Friday's LME bounce.

Common thread: the exchange price corrected ~20% but the premium complex says the physical market is still short units — Japan settled UP for Q3 while EGA runs only ~7% of Al Taweelah pots and exports from inventory. Premium risk stays skewed higher until Gulf capacity visibly rebuilds.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t) easing on Q2/H2 oversupply; API region ~$304 (FOB futures ~$309). Surplus building into H2; the Hormuz reopening removes the Gulf-logistics risk overlay.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38) — Guinea's export-curb push (targeting >$100/dmt) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite while caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 with Gulf logistics risk now receding.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,366/t, ~$6.06/lb, 30-Jun official, steady on firm China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; fell -1.4% on 03-Jul to 2,490 — a SIXTH straight decline and the lowest since 15-Apr (capesize -2.8% to 3,538; panamax +0.7% to 2,124; supramax 1,668). Softer dry-bulk costs ease raw-material freight (Cyprus Shipping News / Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Open — crude flows >10 mbpd; UAE exports >3.9 mbpd
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. Flows are restored (UAE >3.9 mbpd; total >10 mbpd) and the market prices ~700kt of Gulf aluminium returning in H2. Reopening remains 'patchy and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights via Reuters), so residual two-sided headline risk persists — directional read stays bearish for premia.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia continue to normalise as transit holds; keep cover in place while Iran contests maritime administrative control in case of re-escalation.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — retain as a live contingency while Gulf export volumes rebuild toward normal.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing fully normalises.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,250/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics is NORMAL and getting cheaper: Hormuz transit restored (>10 mbpd) and the BDI has slid six straight sessions to 2,490 (lowest since 15-Apr) — bulk freight for bauxite/alumina is easing; keep war-risk cover on standby.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (early-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and an ABC correction is underway. Wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low; wave B failed at $3,406 (22-Jun official). Wave C probed the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50–.618 span / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree) and printed a possible terminal low at $3,061.5 (02-Jul): Friday's close back above the .50 ($3,080 cash / $3,087 3M) with compressing daily ranges is the first basing evidence, but one up-day does not confirm completion. Base case (~50%): C completed or completes marginally lower within the zone; confirmation needs a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) and then a weekly close above $3,488 (.236). Bear alternate (~30%): a sustained weekly close below $3,050 extends C to $2,894 (.618). Bull (~20%): a fast reclaim of $3,260/$3,488 on Gulf restart slippage. The bearish-primary count is invalidated on a sustained weekly close above $3,666. Count unchanged from 03-Jul; no re-label. Confidence: medium.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Daily (swing): from the $3,855 top, wave A fell to $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B was sold at the $3,607 spike (22-Jun), and wave C has unwound to $3,061.5 (02-Jul) — right at the .50 retracement ($3,077) of the whole advance. Friday's +0.7% close back above the level ($3,087 3M), the first up-day in five sessions, flags a POSSIBLE C-wave completion; alternatively C makes one more marginal low (~$3,020–3,050) before turning — hence the projected pivot at ~$3,045. The swing roadmap: hold above $3,050 → relief leg toward $3,160–3,200, with $3,260 (.382) the trigger that upgrades the count to a completed correction. Invalidation for the basing view: a daily close below $3,020 (opens $2,894/.618). Fuel for the bounce: sub-300kt stocks, the $395 Q3 MJP settlement and a softer dollar. Confidence: medium-low until $3,260 is reclaimed.

premium_drivers.d: Q3 MJP settled at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32), UP from Q2's $350–353 and well above the ~$275–320 the market expected — buyers dependent on Middle-East metal paid up despite the LME's ~20% correction; initial producer offers were $460–480.

EGA says ~7% of Al Taweelah pots are back online and aims to accelerate restoration (inventories back to normal by end-2026); Hormuz transit is restored and ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed to return in H2 — the war component of premia unwinds only as slowly as pots restart.

50% tariff on full customs value (June-2026 proclamation, eff. 8-Jun-2026) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (~$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + tariff); the tariff-$ component moves mechanically with the LME.
EU import duty at 6% (reverted from 4% on 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM carbon costs → European premiums structurally supported despite soft demand.

so_what.line: Weekend read: Friday's session left the market with a rare split — the LME bounced off the $3,077 halfway-retracement (3M official $3,087, first up-day since 26-Jun) while Japan's Q3 premium settled sharply HIGHER at $395/t, so the exchange says correction but the physical market says tight.
points: Price and stocks both turned supportive into the weekend: 03-Jul officials closed $3,080 cash / $3,087 3M (+0.6/+0.7%), and LME inventory broke below 300kt (298,775 t) for the first time this cycle on steady ~1,500 t/day draws (Westmetall).

The physical signal strengthened: Q3 MJP settled at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32 with at least one Japanese buyer) — up from Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus — implying spot CIF Japan (~$338 last print) converges UP toward the settlement, not down.

Supply is healing but slowly: EGA has ~7% of Al Taweelah pots back online and is exporting from inventories; Hormuz transit has resumed. The gradual Gulf return caps rallies, but it erodes the deficit over quarters, not days.

Week ahead: Monday's LME reopen tests whether the $3,077 hold was a base or a pause; US markets return from the July-4 weekend with September hike odds ~50% post-payrolls (DXY ~100.8); watch the first CIF Japan spot prints after the $395 settlement and SHFE's Monday open (~RMB 22,600 Friday).

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
